refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature inventory per zone via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a hierarchical zone-feature registry with versioning and access control tied to operational policies.

DP3: Establish a real-time spatial streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies feature extraction and filtering rules (derived from safety plan), and publishes validated feature updates to a shared model store accessible via standardized query interface.

DP4: Capture worksite features as a rule-based declarative model (e.g., constraint satisfaction or logic program) where features are defined by spatial predicates and safety/operational constraints; allow planning/execution to query and reason over the model.

DP5: Implement a hybrid approach: maintain a persistent baseline spatial model (DP1-style graph) updated periodically, overlaid with a dynamic observation layer (DP3-style streaming) for real-time feature state; reconcile via conflict resolution rules aligned with safety policies.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid persistent baseline + dynamic observation layer with policy-aligned reconciliation. Rationale: (1) Decouples long-term spatial structure (baseline model) from transient feature state (observations), reducing coupling between acquisition and planning. (2) Supports both batch planning (baseline) and reactive execution (dynamic layer). (3) Explicit reconciliation rules create a clear integration point for safety/operational policy constraints. (4) Allows graceful degradation: planning can operate on baseline if real-time layer is unavailable.

potential_sources: Baseline model update frequency and latency may create stale-data coupling with planning if not explicitly bounded.
Reconciliation rule complexity could tightly couple safety policy logic to the spatial model; recommend externalizing rules to a policy service.
Real-time observation layer may create tight temporal coupling with execution if latency SLAs are not contractually defined.
Feature classification schema must be agreed between acquisition and planning; schema evolution could break both layers if not versioned.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is superior to DP1–DP4 individually because it isolates concerns (structure vs. state, batch vs. real-time) and provides explicit seams for policy injection. However, the mapping is only high-quality if the baseline-observation reconciliation protocol and feature schema are treated as first-class design artifacts with clear ownership and versioning.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable enough to be encoded as reconciliation rules; RISK: Frequent policy changes will require model updates, creating maintenance burden.
ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate baseline model staleness (e.g., 5–30 min); RISK: If real-time accuracy is critical for safety, baseline-only fallback may be unsafe.
ASSUMPTION: Feature classification taxonomy is agreed and stable across acquisition and planning; RISK: Misalignment in feature semantics (e.g., 'obstacle' vs. 'hazard') will cause planning failures.
ASSUMPTION: Spatial coordinate frame is consistent across all sensing modalities; RISK: Calibration drift or multi-sensor fusion errors could corrupt the baseline model.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria ('agreed set of site features') implies a pre-defined feature inventory; RISK: If feature set is open-ended or context-dependent, the model schema must support extensibility without breaking existing consumers.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Baseline model update cadence and acceptable staleness window. (2) Real-time observation latency and accuracy SLAs. (3) Feature classification taxonomy and versioning strategy. (4) Policy reconciliation rule ownership and change control process. (5) Fallback behavior if real-time layer is unavailable. These refinements will enable DP5 to be decomposed into independent IM layers (baseline store, observation stream, reconciliation engine, query interface) with clear contracts and ownership boundaries.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized state management layer integrating persistent baseline spatial model with dynamic observation layer, including policy-aligned reconciliation logic and graceful degradation mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time sensor observations and feature state updates from Vehicle; feeds dynamic observation layer.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs reconciled planning directives (baseline + dynamic layer consensus) to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety/operational policy constraints; outputs reconciliation decisions and degradation status when real-time layer unavailable.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports distributed baseline model synchronization and observation layer updates across multi-agent or edge scenarios.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for hybrid state management, baseline model persistence, dynamic observation reconciliation, and policy integration. Baseline model (long-term spatial structure) and observation layer (transient features) are both core to NGHE's planning and reasoning. Reconciliation rules align with NGHE's decision logic. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry ingress and Tasking egress cross boundary; SafetyAlert and Network Link are well-scoped policy/sync interfaces.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a reasoning/state-management layer. Placing hybrid baseline + observation reconciliation in Vehicle would couple low-level control with high-level planning logic, violate separation of concerns, and force Vehicle to maintain persistent spatial models and policy reconciliation—tasks that belong in planning/decision logic. High diffusion: baseline model, observation layer, and reconciliation rules would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface, not a state/planning layer. Placing hybrid baseline + observation reconciliation here would conflate operator directives with autonomous reasoning, obscure policy constraints, and create tight coupling between human input and persistent model state. High diffusion: baseline model and reconciliation logic would need to diffuse into NGHE Solution and Safety Services.

Weak fit. Safety Services is a constraint/validation layer, not a state-management layer. While reconciliation rules are policy-aligned, the hybrid baseline + observation layer is a core planning/reasoning construct that belongs upstream in NGHE Solution. Placing it in Safety Services would invert the dependency: planning would depend on safety validation for state management, creating circular coupling. Moderate-to-high diffusion: baseline model and observation layer would leak into NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid persistent baseline + dynamic observation layer with policy-aligned reconciliation) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE Solution is the designated planning and reasoning layer responsible for maintaining spatial models and decision logic; (2) the baseline model (long-term structure) and observation layer (transient state) are both planning-domain artifacts; (3) reconciliation rules are decision-logic constructs that integrate policy constraints at the planning level, not at execution or validation; (4) graceful degradation (fallback to baseline when real-time unavailable) is a planning-level resilience pattern. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout, Network Link inout) are clean and minimal, with no major diffusion into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: If baseline model becomes very large or updates frequently, NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement lazy-loading and incremental update patterns within NGHE Solution; use Network Link for distributed caching if needed.

Risk: Policy reconciliation logic may grow complex and entangle with core planning algorithms. Mitigation: Isolate reconciliation rules in a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution with clear interfaces to baseline and observation layers.

Risk: Real-time observation layer may have latency requirements that conflict with batch baseline planning cycles. Mitigation: Decouple update frequencies; use asynchronous event-driven patterns within NGHE Solution to handle dynamic observations without blocking baseline planning.

Risk: Safety Services may need to veto or override reconciliation decisions at runtime. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface supports bidirectional feedback; reconciliation logic should query Safety Services before committing to Tasking output.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) 'A worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' maps to the hybrid baseline + observation layer output; (2) 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies' maps to policy-aligned reconciliation rules. The NGHE Solution IM boundary explicitly owns both the baseline model persistence and dynamic observation layer, making verification of model generation and availability traceable to a single module. Graceful degradation (fallback to baseline when real-time unavailable) is a testable property of the IM.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR->DP->IM chain is clear: FR defines the problem (capture and represent features); DP5 specifies the solution approach (hybrid persistent + dynamic with reconciliation); NGHE Solution IM is the unambiguous owner. The four external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout, Network Link inout) are well-scoped and create clean boundaries. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for baseline model maintenance, observation layer updates, or reconciliation logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid persistent baseline + dynamic observation layer with policy-aligned reconciliation) directly addresses the FR. The rationale is sound: (1) decoupling long-term structure from transient state reduces coupling between acquisition and planning, supporting the 'machine-readable model' requirement; (2) dual-layer design supports both batch planning and reactive execution, enabling availability to 'planning/execution functions'; (3) explicit reconciliation rules integrate 'worksite safety plan and operational policies' into the model; (4) graceful degradation ensures model availability even under real-time layer failure. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified; it provides sufficient logical structure to guide IM design without prescribing implementation details.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Baseline model size/update frequency may create bottleneck in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Implement lazy-loading and incremental update patterns; use Network Link for distributed caching if multi-agent scenarios require it. Verify that baseline model updates do not block real-time observation layer ingestion.

Moderate risk: Policy reconciliation logic may grow complex and entangle with core planning algorithms. Mitigation: Isolate reconciliation rules in a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution (e.g., 'PolicyReconciliationEngine') with explicit interfaces to baseline and observation layers. Define clear ownership boundaries within NGHE Solution.

Moderate risk: Real-time observation layer latency requirements may conflict with batch baseline planning cycles. Mitigation: Decouple update frequencies using asynchronous event-driven patterns within NGHE Solution. Ensure dynamic observations do not block baseline planning; use eventual consistency model if appropriate.

Low-to-moderate risk: SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional (inout), creating potential for circular dependency if Safety Services queries NGHE Solution for reconciliation status while NGHE Solution queries Safety Services for policy constraints. Mitigation: Define clear request/response semantics; ensure SafetyAlert interface is used for constraint input and status output only, not for real-time veto loops. If runtime veto is required, implement as a separate 'SafetyOverride' interface with explicit priority semantics.

Low risk: Network Link interface for distributed baseline model synchronization may introduce consistency challenges in multi-agent scenarios. Mitigation: Define synchronization protocol (e.g., eventual consistency, version vectors) and document assumptions about network latency and partition tolerance.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08) is the correct and natural home for DP5. The hybrid baseline + observation layer is a planning-domain construct, not an execution or validation construct. Baseline model (long-term spatial structure) and observation layer (transient feature state) are both core to planning and reasoning. Reconciliation rules are decision-logic artifacts that belong upstream of Safety Services (which validates, not generates, decisions). The four external interfaces are minimal and well-scoped: Telemetry ingress and Tasking egress are clean data flows; SafetyAlert and Network Link are policy/sync interfaces that do not leak core logic. No significant diffusion into Vehicle (execution platform), Teleoperation (human interface), or Safety Services (validation layer). The module boundary is architecturally sound.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Within NGHE Solution, create an explicit sub-module 'PolicyReconciliationEngine' to isolate reconciliation rules from baseline and observation layer logic. Define clear interfaces: (a) receives baseline model state and dynamic observations as inputs; (b) queries SafetyAlert for policy constraints; (c) outputs reconciled planning directives to Tasking. This prevents entanglement and improves testability.

Revision 2: Define asynchronous update semantics for the hybrid baseline + observation layer. Specify: (a) baseline model update frequency (e.g., batch cycle); (b) observation layer update frequency (e.g., real-time event-driven); (c) reconciliation trigger (e.g., on observation arrival, on baseline update, or periodic). Document assumptions about latency and eventual consistency.

Revision 3: Clarify SafetyAlert interface semantics to prevent circular dependency. Specify: (a) SafetyAlert is used for constraint input (Safety Services -> NGHE Solution) and status output (NGHE Solution -> Safety Services); (b) if runtime veto is required, define a separate 'SafetyOverride' interface with explicit priority and fallback semantics; (c) document whether reconciliation logic must query Safety Services synchronously or can assume constraints are pre-loaded.

Revision 4: Add acceptance test criteria for graceful degradation: (a) when real-time observation layer is unavailable, planning must fall back to baseline model within [X] seconds; (b) Tasking output must indicate degradation status (baseline-only vs. hybrid); (c) baseline model must remain available and consistent during observation layer outages. Verify these properties in NGHE Solution integration tests.

Revision 5: Document baseline model scope and update protocol for Network Link interface. Specify: (a) which spatial features are included in baseline model; (b) synchronization frequency and consistency model (e.g., eventual consistency, version vectors); (c) conflict resolution strategy if baseline model is updated from multiple sources (e.g., multi-agent scenarios). Ensure Network Link does not introduce consistency violations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. Verification feasibility is high, allocation clarity is excellent, and containment is sound (0.92 score). However, the gate results identify five moderate-to-low diffusion risks and recommend five explicit revisions that require sub-level decomposition to resolve. Specifically: (1) PolicyReconciliationEngine must be isolated as a distinct sub-module with clear interfaces to prevent entanglement with baseline/observation logic; (2) asynchronous update semantics and reconciliation triggers require explicit sub-FR decomposition to specify timing and consistency guarantees; (3) SafetyAlert circular dependency risk requires sub-level interface specification; (4) graceful degradation acceptance criteria require sub-FR definition for fallback behavior; (5) baseline model scope and Network Link synchronization protocol require sub-level specification. These are not revisions to the current FR/DP/IM (which are sound), but rather architectural elaborations that belong at the next decomposition level. Proceeding to sub-FRs will make these risks explicit, traceable, and testable. Safety criticality (high) reinforces the need for explicit sub-level specification of reconciliation logic, fallback semantics, and policy constraint handling.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Maintain persistent baseline spatial model with defined update frequency and consistency guarantees, decoupled from real-time observation layer ingestion.
FR5.2: Ingest, validate, and layer dynamic spatial observations onto baseline model with real-time event-driven semantics, without blocking baseline planning cycles.
FR5.3: Reconcile baseline model state and dynamic observations against worksite safety policies and operational constraints, producing policy-aligned planning directives.
FR5.4: Degrade gracefully to baseline-only planning when real-time observation layer is unavailable, with explicit status indication to Tasking and fallback timing guarantees.
FR5.5: Synchronize baseline spatial model across distributed agents via Network Link with defined consistency model and conflict resolution strategy.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound and passes the FR-DP gate. However, the recommended revisions (especially Revision 1: PolicyReconciliationEngine isolation, Revision 2: asynchronous update semantics, Revision 3: SafetyAlert interface clarification, Revision 4: graceful degradation acceptance criteria, Revision 5: Network Link synchronization protocol) should be incorporated into the DP narrative to make the solution approach more explicit about sub-component responsibilities and interface semantics. This will reduce ambiguity when sub-FRs are decomposed and will improve traceability from sub-DP to sub-IM. Recommend updating DP5 to explicitly mention: (a) PolicyReconciliationEngine as a logical sub-component; (b) asynchronous event-driven update model with defined trigger semantics; (c) SafetyAlert constraint input and status output semantics; (d) fallback to baseline-only mode with timing and status indication; (e) Network Link synchronization protocol assumptions. These additions do not change the DP's core logic but make its architectural intent clearer for sub-level decomposition.

assumptions: Baseline spatial model update frequency is batch-oriented (e.g., periodic or on-demand), not real-time event-driven.
Real-time observation layer can operate asynchronously from baseline model updates without requiring synchronous consistency.
SafetyAlert interface is used for constraint input (Safety Services -> NGHE Solution) and status output (NGHE Solution -> Safety Services), not for real-time veto loops.
Graceful degradation to baseline-only planning is acceptable when real-time observation layer is unavailable, with explicit status indication to Tasking.
Network Link synchronization uses eventual consistency model; multi-agent baseline model conflicts are resolved via version vectors or timestamp-based precedence.
PolicyReconciliationEngine is a logical sub-module within NGHE Solution; its implementation may be a dedicated class, service, or rule engine depending on complexity.
Observation layer latency requirements are compatible with asynchronous event-driven patterns; no hard real-time constraints require synchronous blocking.
Baseline model size and update frequency do not create bottlenecks in NGHE Solution; lazy-loading and incremental updates are feasible.
open_risks: PolicyReconciliationEngine complexity may grow beyond initial scope if safety policies are numerous or interdependent. Mitigation: Define policy constraint schema and reconciliation rule language upfront; isolate policy logic in dedicated sub-module with clear versioning and testing strategy.

Asynchronous update semantics may introduce eventual consistency windows where baseline and observation layers are out-of-sync. Mitigation: Define acceptable staleness bounds for planning decisions; document assumptions about observation layer latency and baseline update frequency.

SafetyAlert circular dependency risk persists if reconciliation logic must query Safety Services synchronously for constraint validation. Mitigation: Implement constraint pre-loading or caching within NGHE Solution; define clear request/response semantics for SafetyAlert interface.

Graceful degradation fallback timing may be difficult to guarantee if baseline model is large or update-heavy. Mitigation: Implement lazy-loading and incremental baseline model access; define fallback timeout and document acceptable planning quality degradation.

Network Link synchronization may introduce consistency violations if baseline model is updated from multiple agents concurrently. Mitigation: Define synchronization protocol (e.g., version vectors, timestamp-based precedence) and test multi-agent scenarios.

Real-time observation layer may introduce latency spikes that violate planning cycle timing. Mitigation: Decouple observation layer update frequency from planning cycle frequency; use asynchronous event-driven patterns and document acceptable observation staleness.
evidence_needed: Sub-level FR decomposition (FR5.1-FR5.5) with explicit DP specifications for baseline model maintenance, observation layer ingestion, reconciliation logic, graceful degradation, and Network Link synchronization.
PolicyReconciliationEngine interface specification: inputs (baseline model state, dynamic observations, policy constraints), outputs (reconciled planning directives), and error handling.
Asynchronous update semantics specification: baseline model update frequency, observation layer update frequency, reconciliation trigger conditions, and acceptable staleness bounds.
SafetyAlert interface clarification: constraint input format, status output format, synchronous vs. asynchronous semantics, and fallback behavior if Safety Services is unavailable.
Graceful degradation acceptance test criteria: fallback timing guarantees, baseline-only planning quality metrics, status indication format, and integration test scenarios.

Network Link synchronization protocol specification: consistency model (eventual consistency, version vectors, timestamp-based precedence), conflict resolution strategy, and multi-agent test scenarios.
Baseline model scope definition: which spatial features are included, update protocol (batch vs. incremental), and size/performance constraints.

Integration test plan for NGHE Solution: baseline model persistence, observation layer ingestion, reconciliation logic, graceful degradation, and Network Link synchronization under nominal and degraded conditions.
